instruction: Implement branded types in TypeScript to create type-safe identifiers (UserId, OrderId, Email) that prevent accidental mixing even though they are all strings at runtime.
output:
<think>
TypeScript's structural type system means two types with the same shape are interchangeable. A UserId (string) and an OrderId (string) are the same type to TypeScript. This causes real bugs when you pass a user ID where an order ID is expected.

Branded types solve this by adding a phantom property that exists only in the type system, not at runtime. The brand is a unique symbol or literal type that distinguishes otherwise identical types.

Two approaches:
1. Intersection with a branded interface: `type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' }`
2. Opaque type via unique symbol: more robust, prevents brand forging

The unique symbol approach is stronger because you can't accidentally create the brand — only the constructor function can. The intersection approach is simpler but technically forgeable.

Design:
- Define Brand<K, T> utility type
- Create constructor functions that validate and cast
- TypeScript erases the brand at runtime (zero cost)
- Functions that accept UserId reject OrderId at compile time

This pattern is used in production by companies like Stripe and Bloomberg for their TypeScript codebases. It catches a whole class of ID-mixing bugs without any runtime overhead.
</think>

```typescript
// --- Brand utility ---
declare const __brand: unique symbol;
type Brand<K extends string, T> = T & { readonly [__brand]: K };

// --- Branded types ---
type UserId = Brand<'UserId', string>;
type OrderId = Brand<'OrderId', string>;
type Email = Brand<'Email', string>;
type Cents = Brand<'Cents', number>;
type Dollars = Brand<'Dollars', number>;

// --- Smart constructors with validation ---

function createUserId(id: string): UserId {
  if (!id.startsWith('usr_')) {
    throw new Error(`Invalid user ID format: ${id}`);
  }
  return id as UserId;
}

function createOrderId(id: string): OrderId {
  if (!id.startsWith('ord_')) {
    throw new Error(`Invalid order ID format: ${id}`);
  }
  return id as OrderId;
}

function createEmail(email: string): Email {
  if (!email.includes('@') || !email.includes('.')) {
    throw new Error(`Invalid email: ${email}`);
  }
  return email as Email;
}

function toCents(dollars: number): Cents {
  return Math.round(dollars * 100) as Cents;
}

function toDollars(cents: Cents): Dollars {
  return (cents / 100) as Dollars;
}

// --- Domain functions with type-safe signatures ---

interface Order {
  id: OrderId;
  userId: UserId;
  email: Email;
  totalCents: Cents;
}

function getUser(userId: UserId): { name: string; email: Email } {
  return { name: 'Alice', email: createEmail('alice@example.com') };
}

function getOrder(orderId: OrderId): Order {
  return {
    id: orderId,
    userId: createUserId('usr_123'),
    email: createEmail('alice@example.com'),
    totalCents: toCents(49.99),
  };
}

function sendReceipt(email: Email, order: OrderId): void {
  console.log(`Sending receipt for ${order} to ${email}`);
}

// --- Usage ---
const userId = createUserId('usr_123');
const orderId = createOrderId('ord_456');
const email = createEmail('alice@example.com');

getUser(userId);       // OK
getOrder(orderId);     // OK
sendReceipt(email, orderId); // OK

// These would cause compile errors:
// getUser(orderId);     // Error: OrderId is not assignable to UserId
// getOrder(userId);     // Error: UserId is not assignable to OrderId
// sendReceipt(userId, orderId); // Error: UserId is not assignable to Email

// Money safety:
const price = toCents(29.99);
const display = toDollars(price);
// toDollars(42);  // Error: number is not assignable to Cents

console.log(`User: ${userId}, Order: ${orderId}, Price: $${display}`);
```

instruction: Implement a type-safe builder pattern in TypeScript that uses the type system to enforce that required fields are set before build() can be called.
output:
<think>
The builder pattern in TypeScript can leverage mapped types and conditional types to make build() only available when all required fields have been set. This is similar to Rust's typestate pattern but achieved through TypeScript's type-level programming.

Approach:
1. Define the target type with required and optional fields
2. The builder tracks which required fields have been set via a type parameter (a record of boolean flags)
3. Each setter method returns a new builder type with that field's flag set to true
4. build() is only available when all required flags are true

The key technique: use a type parameter like `Set extends Record<string, boolean>` to track state. Each setter changes one flag from false to true. build() has a conditional type that only resolves when all required flags are true.

Alternative: use phantom types with intersection. Start with Builder<never>, and each setter adds to the phantom type. build() requires the phantom to include all required keys.

I'll use the phantom intersection approach as it's more elegant:
- Builder<T> where T accumulates set field names
- set(key) returns Builder<T | key>
- build() requires T extends RequiredKeys

This gives compile-time errors for missing fields with zero runtime overhead.
</think>

```typescript
// --- Target type ---
interface ServerConfig {
  host: string;       // required
  port: number;       // required
  protocol: string;   // required
  maxConn?: number;   // optional
  timeout?: number;   // optional
  tls?: boolean;      // optional
}

// --- Type-safe builder ---

type RequiredKeys = 'host' | 'port' | 'protocol';

class ServerConfigBuilder<Set extends string = never> {
  private config: Partial<ServerConfig> = {};

  host(value: string): ServerConfigBuilder<Set | 'host'> {
    this.config.host = value;
    return this as any;
  }

  port(value: number): ServerConfigBuilder<Set | 'port'> {
    this.config.port = value;
    return this as any;
  }

  protocol(value: 'http' | 'https'): ServerConfigBuilder<Set | 'protocol'> {
    this.config.protocol = value;
    return this as any;
  }

  // Optional fields don't change the type parameter
  maxConnections(value: number): this {
    this.config.maxConn = value;
    return this;
  }

  timeout(value: number): this {
    this.config.timeout = value;
    return this;
  }

  tls(value: boolean): this {
    this.config.tls = value;
    return this;
  }

  // build() only available when all required keys are set
  build(this: ServerConfigBuilder<RequiredKeys>): ServerConfig {
    return this.config as ServerConfig;
  }
}

// --- Generic version for any type ---

type AllRequired<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]-?: undefined extends T[K] ? never : K;
}[keyof T];

function createBuilder<T>() {
  type Req = AllRequired<T>;
  const data: Partial<T> = {};

  const handler: ProxyHandler<any> = {
    get(_target, prop: string) {
      if (prop === 'build') {
        return () => ({ ...data }) as T;
      }
      return (value: any) => {
        (data as any)[prop] = value;
        return new Proxy({}, handler);
      };
    },
  };

  return new Proxy({}, handler) as {
    [K in keyof T]-?: (value: T[K]) => any;
  } & { build(): T };
}

// --- Usage ---

// Type-safe: all required fields must be set
const config = new ServerConfigBuilder()
  .host('0.0.0.0')
  .port(8080)
  .protocol('https')
  .timeout(30000)
  .build(); // OK: all required fields set

console.log(config);
// { host: '0.0.0.0', port: 8080, protocol: 'https', timeout: 30000 }

// This would cause a compile error:
// new ServerConfigBuilder()
//   .host('0.0.0.0')
//   .port(8080)
//   .build(); // Error: 'protocol' not set

// Order doesn't matter:
const config2 = new ServerConfigBuilder()
  .protocol('http')
  .port(3000)
  .host('localhost')
  .maxConnections(100)
  .build(); // OK

console.log(config2);
```

instruction: Implement exhaustive pattern matching in TypeScript using discriminated unions and the never type to ensure all cases are handled at compile time.
output:
<think>
TypeScript's discriminated unions combined with the `never` type provide compile-time exhaustiveness checking. When a switch statement handles all variants of a union, the default case receives `never`. If you add a new variant but forget to handle it, TypeScript will error because the new variant is not assignable to `never`.

This is TypeScript's equivalent of Rust's match or Haskell's case — the compiler ensures you handle every possibility.

Design:
1. Define a discriminated union with a literal `type` discriminant
2. Write handler functions with switch on the discriminant
3. Use an `assertNever(x: never)` function in the default case
4. If you add a new variant, the compiler forces you to add a handler

The assertNever function is the key trick: it takes `never` as a parameter, so if TypeScript can prove a value reaches it, it means we missed a case. At runtime, it throws for safety.

I'll also show a more advanced pattern: a match() utility function that takes an object with handlers for each variant, providing a more functional style.

Edge cases: what about generic discriminated unions? The pattern works the same way. What about deeply nested unions? You can compose matchers.
</think>

```typescript
// --- Exhaustive check utility ---
function assertNever(x: never, msg?: string): never {
  throw new Error(msg ?? `Unexpected value: ${JSON.stringify(x)}`);
}

// --- Discriminated union: Shape ---
interface Circle {
  type: 'circle';
  radius: number;
}

interface Rectangle {
  type: 'rectangle';
  width: number;
  height: number;
}

interface Triangle {
  type: 'triangle';
  base: number;
  height: number;
}

type Shape = Circle | Rectangle | Triangle;

// Exhaustive handler — add a new Shape variant and this breaks
function area(shape: Shape): number {
  switch (shape.type) {
    case 'circle':
      return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2;
    case 'rectangle':
      return shape.width * shape.height;
    case 'triangle':
      return (shape.base * shape.height) / 2;
    default:
      return assertNever(shape); // Compile error if case missing
  }
}

// --- Functional match utility ---

type MatchHandlers<U extends { type: string }, R> = {
  [K in U['type']]: (value: Extract<U, { type: K }>) => R;
};

function match<U extends { type: string }, R>(
  value: U,
  handlers: MatchHandlers<U, R>
): R {
  const handler = (handlers as any)[value.type];
  if (!handler) {
    throw new Error(`No handler for type: ${value.type}`);
  }
  return handler(value);
}

// Usage with match()
const describe = (shape: Shape): string =>
  match(shape, {
    circle: (s) => `Circle with radius ${s.radius}`,
    rectangle: (s) => `${s.width}x${s.height} rectangle`,
    triangle: (s) => `Triangle with base ${s.base}`,
    // Remove any handler and TypeScript will error
  });

// --- More complex example: Result type ---

type Result<T, E> =
  | { type: 'ok'; value: T }
  | { type: 'err'; error: E };

function ok<T>(value: T): Result<T, never> {
  return { type: 'ok', value };
}

function err<E>(error: E): Result<never, E> {
  return { type: 'err', error };
}

function unwrap<T, E>(result: Result<T, E>): T {
  switch (result.type) {
    case 'ok':
      return result.value;
    case 'err':
      throw new Error(`Unwrap on error: ${result.error}`);
    default:
      return assertNever(result);
  }
}

function mapResult<T, U, E>(
  result: Result<T, E>,
  fn: (value: T) => U
): Result<U, E> {
  return match(result, {
    ok: (r) => ok(fn(r.value)),
    err: (r) => err(r.error),
  });
}

// --- Demo ---
const shapes: Shape[] = [
  { type: 'circle', radius: 5 },
  { type: 'rectangle', width: 10, height: 3 },
  { type: 'triangle', base: 8, height: 6 },
];

for (const s of shapes) {
  console.log(`${describe(s)}: area = ${area(s).toFixed(2)}`);
}

const result: Result<number, string> = ok(42);
console.log(unwrap(mapResult(result, (x) => x * 2))); // 84
```

instruction: Build a type-safe event bus in TypeScript where event types are defined as a map, and subscribers receive correctly typed payloads with full autocomplete support.
output:
<think>
A type-safe event bus ensures that when you emit 'user:login', the payload must match the defined type for that event, and subscribers receive the correctly typed payload. This catches event name typos and payload shape mismatches at compile time.

Design:
1. Define an EventMap interface where keys are event names and values are payload types
2. The EventBus class is generic over the EventMap
3. on<K extends keyof EventMap>(event: K, handler: (payload: EventMap[K]) => void)
4. emit<K extends keyof EventMap>(event: K, payload: EventMap[K])
5. TypeScript infers the payload type from the event name

Advanced features:
- Wildcard listeners (on('*', handler)) for logging/debugging
- Once listeners that auto-remove after first call
- Typed return values from handlers for request/response patterns
- Off method returns a cleanup function (React hook friendly)

The key insight is that TypeScript's mapped types and indexed access types (EventMap[K]) let us tie the event name to its payload type in a single generic parameter. The compiler then enforces correctness everywhere.

This pattern is used in frameworks like Mitt, but with our own implementation we get more control and can add features like priority, async handlers, and typed responses.
</think>

```typescript
// --- Event map definition ---
interface AppEvents {
  'user:login': { userId: string; timestamp: number };
  'user:logout': { userId: string; reason: string };
  'order:created': { orderId: string; items: string[]; total: number };
  'order:shipped': { orderId: string; trackingId: string };
  'notification': { message: string; level: 'info' | 'warn' | 'error' };
}

// --- Type-safe event bus ---

type Handler<T> = (payload: T) => void;

class TypedEventBus<Events extends Record<string, any>> {
  private handlers = new Map<keyof Events, Set<Handler<any>>>();
  private onceHandlers = new WeakSet<Handler<any>>();
  private wildcardHandlers = new Set<Handler<{ event: keyof Events; payload: any }>>();

  on<K extends keyof Events>(event: K, handler: Handler<Events[K]>): () => void {
    if (!this.handlers.has(event)) {
      this.handlers.set(event, new Set());
    }
    this.handlers.get(event)!.add(handler);

    // Return unsubscribe function
    return () => this.off(event, handler);
  }

  once<K extends keyof Events>(event: K, handler: Handler<Events[K]>): () => void {
    this.onceHandlers.add(handler);
    return this.on(event, handler);
  }

  off<K extends keyof Events>(event: K, handler: Handler<Events[K]>): void {
    this.handlers.get(event)?.delete(handler);
  }

  emit<K extends keyof Events>(event: K, payload: Events[K]): void {
    const handlers = this.handlers.get(event);
    if (handlers) {
      for (const handler of handlers) {
        handler(payload);
        if (this.onceHandlers.has(handler)) {
          handlers.delete(handler);
          this.onceHandlers.delete(handler);
        }
      }
    }

    // Notify wildcard listeners
    for (const handler of this.wildcardHandlers) {
      handler({ event, payload });
    }
  }

  onAll(handler: Handler<{ event: keyof Events; payload: any }>): () => void {
    this.wildcardHandlers.add(handler);
    return () => this.wildcardHandlers.delete(handler);
  }

  listenerCount<K extends keyof Events>(event: K): number {
    return this.handlers.get(event)?.size ?? 0;
  }

  clear(): void {
    this.handlers.clear();
    this.wildcardHandlers.clear();
  }
}

// --- Usage ---

const bus = new TypedEventBus<AppEvents>();

// Full autocomplete on event names and typed payloads
const unsub = bus.on('user:login', (payload) => {
  // payload is { userId: string; timestamp: number }
  console.log(`User ${payload.userId} logged in at ${payload.timestamp}`);
});

bus.on('order:created', (payload) => {
  // payload is { orderId: string; items: string[]; total: number }
  console.log(`Order ${payload.orderId}: ${payload.items.length} items, $${payload.total}`);
});

// One-time listener
bus.once('notification', (payload) => {
  console.log(`[${payload.level}] ${payload.message}`);
});

// Wildcard for debugging
bus.onAll(({ event, payload }) => {
  console.log(`[DEBUG] ${String(event)}:`, payload);
});

// Emit with type-safe payloads
bus.emit('user:login', { userId: 'u_123', timestamp: Date.now() });
bus.emit('order:created', { orderId: 'ord_456', items: ['widget', 'gadget'], total: 59.98 });
bus.emit('notification', { message: 'System ready', level: 'info' });

// These would cause compile errors:
// bus.emit('user:login', { userId: 123 });    // number not assignable to string
// bus.emit('typo:event', {});                 // 'typo:event' not in AppEvents
// bus.emit('order:created', { orderId: 'x' }); // missing 'items' and 'total'

// Cleanup
unsub();
console.log(`Login listeners: ${bus.listenerCount('user:login')}`);
```
